questions.text: Which of the following are advantages of hysteroscopy over blind endometrial sampling for evaluation of abnormal uterine bleeding? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Improved detection of focal lesions
B) Lower cost in all settings
D) More accurate sampling in postmenopausal patients

questions.explanation: Hysteroscopy allows direct visualization, improves detection of focal pathology, and offers superior accuracy in postmenopausal bleeding. It may require dilation and can be more costly than office biopsy.
questions.submodule: Module 9.1 – Hysteroscopic Evaluation of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding

questions.text: Which 2 findings raise the strongest concern for post-ablation tubal ligation syndrome?
questions.explanation: Cornual dilation plus prior tubal ligation is classic for PATLS.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about submucosal fibroid classification are correct? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) ESGE classifies fibroids by size and vascularity
B) Lasmar STEP-W includes fibroid penetration and wall location
C) Type 2 fibroids require laparoscopy for complete removal
D) Type 0 fibroids are completely intracavitary
E) ESGE classifies fibroids as Type 0, 1, or 2

questions.explanation: ESGE uses depth of myometrial invasion (Types 0–2). Lasmar STEP-W incorporates size, topography, extension/penetration, and wall location. Type 0 fibroids are fully intracavitary.

questions.text: Which 3 preoperative factors are most relevant when determining candidacy for hysteroscopic myomectomy?
questions.explanation: Wall location and intramural extension predict technical feasibility; fertility goals guide conservative vs definitive management.

questions.text: Which of the following are benefits of mechanical tissue extraction for polypectomy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Enables resection under direct vision
C) Lowest recurrence rate among techniques
D) Lower cost than polyp forceps
E) Eliminates need for cervical dilation

questions.explanation: Mechanical systems resect under direct vision and avoid electrosurgery; they have higher up-front costs and may still require dilation.

questions.text: Which 3 complications are most associated with hysteroscopic polypectomy?
questions.explanation: Recognized risks include infection, perforation, and—when hypotonic media are used—hyponatremia.

questions.text: What is a primary limitation of office-based endometrial biopsy in the evaluation of abnormal uterine bleeding?

answerOptions.text: A) It is not tolerated in postmenopausal women
C) It has low sensitivity for focal lesions
D) It is more expensive than hysteroscopy

questions.explanation: Blind sampling underdetects focal pathology such as small cancers or polyps.
questions.submodule: Module 9.1 – Hysteroscopic Evaluation of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding

questions.text: Which patient would benefit most from initial hysteroscopic evaluation over biopsy or D&C?

answerOptions.text: A) Premenopausal woman with diffuse thickening
B) Postmenopausal woman with a focal lesion
D) Young woman with anovulatory bleeding

questions.explanation: Hysteroscopy excels when imaging shows a focal lesion by allowing targeted biopsy/removal.
questions.submodule: Module 9.1 – Hysteroscopic Evaluation of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding

questions.text: Studies show what percentage of patients undergoing D&C prior to hysterectomy had less than half of the endometrium effectively sampled?
questions.explanation: About 60% had under-sampling with blind D&C.
questions.submodule: Module 9.1 – Hysteroscopic Evaluation of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding

questions.text: Which condition is a contraindication to endometrial ablation?
questions.explanation: Hyperplasia (or malignancy) must be excluded and treated—not ablated.

questions.text: A 47-year-old G2P2 underwent endometrial ablation 2 years ago and previously had a postpartum tubal ligation. She now has cyclic cramping and spotting; ultrasound shows a dilated tube and distended cornua. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: PATLS: trapped endometrium/scarred cornua in the setting of prior tubal ligation.

questions.text: What is a potentially fatal complication associated with hypotonic distension media during hysteroscopic procedures?
questions.explanation: Absorption of hypotonic fluids (e.g., glycine) can cause severe hyponatremia and cerebral edema.

questions.text: Which fibroid classification is most suitable for hysteroscopic myomectomy?
questions.explanation: Type 0 fibroids are pedunculated and fully intracavitary—ideal for hysteroscopic removal.

questions.text: What classification system includes size, topography, penetration, and wall location for fibroids?
questions.explanation: Lasmar STEP-W scores Size, Topography, Extension/penetration, and Wall involvement.

questions.text: A Type 2 submucosal fibroid is discovered to abut the uterine serosa. What is the best surgical management?
questions.explanation: When the lesion abuts serosa, hysteroscopic access is limited and perforation risk is high; laparoscopy is safer.

questions.text: Which imaging modality offers the highest diagnostic accuracy for identifying endometrial polyps?
questions.explanation: Hysteroscopy provides direct visualization and allows simultaneous treatment.

questions.text: Which technique for polyp removal carries the highest risk of incomplete resection?
questions.explanation: Blind D&C lacks visualization and often misses the polyp base.

questions.text: During polypectomy using mechanical tissue extraction, where should the resection begin?

answerOptions.text: B) At the base of the polyp
C) At the tip of the polyp

questions.explanation: Start at the tip and work toward the base to minimize bleeding and maintain visualization.
